title: Rocky
commentary: {"overall": "Rocky is the archetypal self-made underdog story: a broke club fighter from South Philly earns his shot through sheer grit, and the film's emotional reward is self-respect and proving he can 'go the distance,' not wealth or status. Every dimension scores near its ceiling because the story is genuinely about an ordinary man's interior life — dignity, love, and identity — rather than ascension or being chosen.", "wealth": "Rocky Balboa is explicitly broke, works as a debt collector, and the story's endorsed reward is not money or status but the personal validation of lasting fifteen rounds — a textbook anchor for the 40-band.", "agency": "Rocky drives every meaningful choice: he accepts the fight, trains obsessively on his own terms, and decides mid-fight to redefine his goal; no one rescues him or solves his story for him.", "core": "The film's true subject is self-worth and the fear of being a 'bum' — Rocky's arc is entirely interior, about proving something to himself rather than winning, which is as honest and deep as the rubric's top band demands.", "music": "The film relies on Bill Conti's iconic instrumental score rather than original songs that carry narrative; licensed/score-only band applies.", "inclusion": "Band 5/10 (Neutral): The cast is a working-class Philadelphia milieu with no meaningful inclusion lesson and no group harmed; non-issue, non-lesson.", "romance": "The relationship with Adrian is central and warmly depicted, but it is a mutual partnership that develops organically — it is not the prize Rocky is chasing, and he is not 'chosen' by her in a status sense; balanced presence warrants a 3."}

title: American History X
commentary: {"overall": "American History X is a structurally serious film about radicalization, deradicalization, and the cost of hate — its emotional core is genuine and its anti-prejudice thesis is the entire engine of the plot. Derek is a working-class nobody whose specialness is entirely self-made (for ill and then for good), and the story is fundamentally about identity, grief, and moral responsibility. The tragic ending reinforces that the lesson is never fully won, which deepens rather than undermines the core.", "wealth": "Derek and Danny are ordinary working-class kids from Venice Beach; no inherited status or wealth does narrative work, and the story's reward is moral redemption rather than status ascension — firmly in the 'genuinely ordinary protagonist' band.", "agency": "Derek drives his own arc through his own choices — joining the movement, committing violence, reconsidering in prison, and actively trying to pull Danny out — but the prison mentor Lamont and Dr. Sweeney do significant structural work in catalyzing his change, splitting the agency enough to pull below 17.", "core": "The film's emotional core is identity, grief (the father's murder as the wound that opens Derek to radicalization), and the question of whether hate can be unlearned — this is deep, honest, and sustained throughout, earning the top band.", "music": "The film uses score and licensed/diegetic music only; no original songs carry the narrative — standard score-only band.", "inclusion": "Band 10/10 (inclusion/anti-prejudice IS the lesson): The entire plot is the mechanics and consequences of white supremacist radicalization and its undoing; anti-prejudice is not a secondary theme but the structural spine of the film.", "romance": "Romance is essentially absent from the narrative structure; it plays no role in the protagonist's arc or reward — scores at the top of this dimension."}

title: Shawshank Redemption
commentary: {"overall": "Shawshank is a story about hope, dignity, and perseverance earned over nearly two decades of patient, deliberate effort — Andy's escape is entirely self-made through ingenuity and will. The emotional core is genuinely profound, but the world is a nearly all-male, predominantly white prison environment that presents that homogeneity as unremarkable default.", "wealth": "Andy was a banker before prison, giving him modest born-into status (financial literacy, composure), but the story's endorsed reward is freedom and friendship, not wealth or status — the escape is earned over decades of grinding effort, placing this squarely in the 38 band alongside the rubric's own anchor score.", "agency": "Andy solves his own story almost entirely — the tunnel, the letters, the evidence, the escape plan are all his — but Red's narration and Red's own arc share meaningful weight, and the warden's eventual exposure is partly luck of timing, pulling this just below a pure 17.", "core": "The film's emotional core is deeply honest: hope as an act of defiance against institutional dehumanization, the cost of institutionalization, and the redemptive power of friendship — this is the rubric's top band without question.", "music": "No original songs; Thomas Newman's score is atmospheric and effective but purely instrumental, placing this in the score-only band.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The prison world is overwhelmingly male and the narrative centers white characters almost exclusively; no group is actively harmed or stereotyped, but the homogeneous world is presented as unremarkable default with no inclusion lesson offered.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent from the narrative; Andy's wife appears only as backstory motivation for his imprisonment, placing this firmly in the 6 band."}

title: Parasite
commentary: {"overall": "Parasite is a savage structural critique of class stratification in which the Kim family's ascension into the Park household is the engine of the plot but is explicitly framed as precarious, delusional, and ultimately catastrophic — the story endorses no one's climb and punishes the fantasy of status ascension with violence and tragedy. The emotional core is genuinely deep: it is about the dignity-destroying geometry of poverty and the invisible violence of wealth. The film scores low on wealth because status-climbing is the entire narrative engine and the endorsed outcome is ruin, not earned livelihood — it sits in the Goodfellas/Scarface band where the pursuit of status is the story's spine even if the ending is punitive.", "wealth": "The Kims infiltrate a wealthy household through fraud and the entire plot is a status-ascension scheme; even though the ending is punitive rather than celebratory, the narrative structure is built around climbing into wealth, placing it firmly in the 0–10 band alongside Goodfellas and Scarface — the critique is present but the story's engine is the endorsed-then-destroyed climb.", "agency": "Ki-woo and the Kim family make active, consequential choices that drive the plot, but the catastrophic climax is partly triggered by the hidden-basement revelation — an external discovery — and the resolution is shaped by forces (Mr. Park's disgust, Moon-gwang's husband) beyond their control, landing in the strong-but-split band.", "core": "The film's emotional core is a rigorous, honest excavation of class shame, aspiration, and the dehumanising invisibility that poverty imposes — Ki-woo's final letter and Ki-taek's disappearance underground are among the most emotionally precise images in recent cinema, earning the top band.", "music": "The film uses a composed orchestral score (Jung Jae-il) effectively but has no original songs and relies on score and selective diegetic music, placing it in the score-only band.", "inclusion": "Band 8/10 (strong inclusion content, secondary to main arc): Class as a form of systemic othering is central and rendered with specificity and empathy — the film humanises the poor without sentimentalising them and indicts the wealthy without caricature — but the primary lesson is class critique rather than a prejudice-as-plot anti-discrimination arc.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent as a narrative goal or reward; no character's arc is resolved through being chosen or married, earning the top band."}

title: Trainspotting
commentary: {"overall": "Trainspotting follows a genuinely ordinary working-class Edinburgh addict who ultimately chooses life and self-determination over the pull of heroin and his toxic social circle. The structure endorses Renton's hard-won, unglamorous escape — a small sum of stolen money representing freedom rather than status — and the emotional core is an unflinching portrait of addiction, identity, and the cost of choosing to live. No inherited specialness, no romantic prize, no status ascension.", "wealth": "Renton is broke, low-status, and the story's endorsed reward is a modest cash escape and personal survival — closer to a livelihood outcome than any status ascension, firmly in the 38-40 band of genuinely ordinary protagonist.", "agency": "Renton drives his own arc — he chooses to get clean, chooses to participate in the drug deal, and crucially chooses to betray his friends and walk away — but the final act is partly luck-assisted (the deal falling into place) and his earlier relapses show agency repeatedly undermined, landing in the strong-but-diluted band.", "core": "The film's emotional core is a serious, honest reckoning with addiction as identity crisis — 'Choose life' is a genuine philosophical provocation — stopping just short of the deepest grief/belonging tier because the interiority, while vivid, is more satirical than emotionally excavated.", "music": "The film uses a celebrated licensed soundtrack (Iggy Pop, Underworld, Blur, Pulp) that is atmospherically integral but not original story-carrying songs; score-only/licensed band applies.", "inclusion": "Band 5/10 (Neutral): The cast is homogeneously white Scottish and no prejudice lesson is present, but no group is actively harmed or stereotyped — passive neutral.", "romance": "Romance is entirely peripheral and never framed as a prize; Renton's brief relationship with Diane is a subplot with no narrative weight as a reward, placing this firmly in the absent/subverted band."}

title: There Will Be Blood
commentary: {"overall": "There Will Be Blood is a portrait of rapacious capitalism and spiritual corruption: Daniel Plainview claws his way from lone prospector to oil baron, and the story unambiguously endorses — or at least glorifies through fascination — the accumulation of wealth and dominance as the defining measure of a man. The emotional core is genuinely profound (greed as self-annihilation, the hollowness of the American Dream), but the structural reward is status and wealth ascension, landing it firmly in the lowest band.", "wealth": "Plainview begins ordinary but the entire narrative arc is the endorsed spectacle of wealth and power accumulation with no meaningful critique that redeems the structure — the 'reward' is his empire, even as it destroys him, placing it near the Scarface/Wolf of Wall Street band (score: 2).", "agency": "Plainview drives virtually every plot development through his own ruthless choices, but the climax is somewhat destiny-of-character rather than a clean problem-solved resolution, and Eli Sunday's parallel arc dilutes the pure agency reading — strong but not a clean 17.", "core": "The film's emotional core is a serious, honest excavation of greed, loneliness, misanthropy, and the corruption of the American self — as deep and unsparing as any in the rubric's top examples.", "music": "Jonny Greenwood's score is a major artistic presence but it is purely instrumental/orchestral with no songs; score-only band applies.", "inclusion": "Band 2/10 (harmful stereotyping present): The film's treatment of evangelical Christianity through Eli Sunday edges into othering and caricature, and the world is an almost entirely white male space presented as default with no counter-framing — passive exclusion plus active mockery of a religious group tips it below neutral.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent from the narrative; Plainview has no romantic arc whatsoever, earning the full subversion score."}

title: The Big Lebowski
commentary: {"overall": "The Dude is a gloriously ordinary, broke slacker whose story is explicitly a satire of wealth and ambition — the 'Big' Lebowski's status is exposed as hollow fraud, and The Dude's reward is simply returning to his bowling and White Russians. Agency is real but diluted by the Coens' labyrinthine plot machinery that frequently overwhelms his choices. The emotional core is genuine but oblique: it's about a man who refuses to be anything other than himself in a world demanding he perform ambition.", "wealth": "Wealth and status are actively critiqued as hollow — the Big Lebowski is a fraud, his fortune is fake, and The Dude's 'reward' is his unchanged, penniless life; this lands squarely in the 30-band 'wealth critiqued as hollow' logic.", "agency": "The Dude makes choices and drives the investigation, but the plot constantly overwhelms him with coincidence, mistaken identity, and forces entirely outside his control — strong but meaningfully diluted, placing him in the 13-band.", "core": "Beneath the noir parody is a real, if understated, meditation on identity, male performance, and the dignity of opting out of ambition — real substance, but delivered obliquely rather than with deep emotional honesty.", "music": "No original story-carrying songs; the film uses a rich licensed soundtrack (Eagles, CCR, Bob Dylan, Creedence) that flavors tone but does not carry narrative — score/licensed band at 5.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The world is overwhelmingly white and male; Maude is a strong female character but Bunny and the nihilist's girlfriend are objectified; no group is actively degraded as spectacle but the homogeneous default and passive female objectification constitute a missed opportunity.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent as a goal or prize — The Dude has a casual sexual encounter with Maude that he is largely passive about, and no relationship is framed as a reward; this is effectively subverted by indifference."}

title: Pulp Fiction
commentary: {"overall": "Pulp Fiction is a non-linear crime anthology whose protagonists are working-level hitmen — not wealthy, not born into greatness — but the story's endorsed outcomes are survival and escape within a criminal status hierarchy, placing it near the bottom of the wealth/status band. Agency is split across multiple protagonists with luck and coincidence doing heavy narrative lifting (the miracle of the missed shots, Butch's chain of accidents). The emotional core has real substance — redemption, fate, loyalty, the question of whether one can walk away — but it is delivered obliquely rather than with the depth of a grief or identity arc.", "wealth": "The protagonists are low-level mob employees and a broke boxer; no one is born into greatness, but survival within and escape from criminal status hierarchy is the endorsed reward, landing this near Goodfellas/Scarface territory (band: 0-6).", "agency": "Jules and Butch both make decisive, character-driven choices at their climaxes, but Vincent's arc is resolved by accident/death, Mia's overdose crisis is solved by others, and divine coincidence (the miracle) is structurally central — strong but meaningfully diluted, placing this in the 13 band.", "core": "There is real thematic substance around redemption, signs from God, and the choice to walk away from violence (Jules's arc especially), but it is fragmented across anthology structure and never achieves the sustained emotional depth of a single honest core arc — solid 13.", "music": "The film uses an iconic licensed soundtrack (surf rock, soul, pop) rather than original songs; the music is atmospheric and character-defining but functions as score/licensed selection, fitting the 5 band.", "inclusion": "Band 2/10 (Harmful stereotyping present): The film contains active racial slurs used repeatedly by white characters framed as edgy rather than condemned, the 'gimp' sequence othering of a disabled/captive figure as spectacle, and Black characters who are disproportionately victimized or sexualized in ways the text does not critique — harmful stereotyping is present in the text.", "romance": "No romance is framed as a prize or endpoint; Mia and Vincent's dynamic is deliberately unconsummated and subverted, and Butch's relationship with Fabienne is incidental to his arc — romance is present but not the reward, closer to the 6 band."}

title: Fight Club
commentary: {"overall": "Fight Club is a deeply thematic film whose emotional core — masculine identity crisis, consumerism as spiritual void, dissociation and self-destruction — is genuinely rich and honest. However, the Narrator's agency is severely compromised by the twist: he has been both protagonist and antagonist without knowing it, meaning the climax is partly solved by revelation rather than earned choice. The film actively critiques wealth and status accumulation, landing it in the 'critiqued as hollow' band.", "wealth": "The entire narrative is a sustained, unambiguous critique of consumer culture and status as spiritual emptiness — the Narrator's IKEA-catalog life is the disease, not the reward — placing this firmly in the 'wealth/status critiqued as hollow' band around 30, nudged slightly up because the protagonist is a salaried everyman, not born into anything.", "agency": "The Narrator does make a final decisive choice (shooting himself to kill Tyler), but the bulk of the story's driving force was enacted by a dissociated alter-ego he didn't know existed, making the solving heavily luck- and revelation-assisted rather than purely self-directed — split/destiny-assisted band.", "core": "The film's emotional core is a genuine, unflinching examination of identity dissolution, masculine purposelessness, and the violence of self-loathing — this is as honest and deep as the top band warrants.", "music": "The Dust Brothers' score is instrumental and atmospheric with no original narrative songs; licensed/score-only band applies.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive exclusion / homogeneous default): The world is almost entirely white and male, women are peripheral or sexualized (Marla is a plot device as much as a person), and no inclusion lesson is present; no active ethnic stereotyping but the passive exclusion and gendered othering of Marla nudge it to the lower end of this band.", "romance": "Marla and the Narrator have a relationship, but it is framed as dysfunction and co-dependency rather than a prize; romance is present but clearly subordinate to the identity/destruction arc — balanced band."}

title: Titanic
commentary: {"overall": "Titanic is structurally a love story in which a poor young man briefly transcends class barriers through charm and luck, but the narrative's endorsed reward is Rose being 'chosen' by Jack and escaping her gilded cage — not through earned transformation but through romantic selection. The emotional core around mortality, identity, and freedom is genuinely deep, but the wealth dimension is complicated by the story glorifying Jack's ascension into first-class spaces and Rose's ultimate liberation being framed as romantic destiny rather than self-made agency.", "wealth": "Jack is nominally poor but the story's engine is his access to wealth/status spaces via luck (winning the ticket) and charm, and the narrative endorses Rose's escape from oppressive wealth through being chosen by the right man — status-as-prize logic places this near the bottom band, not quite Cinderella but structurally adjacent.", "agency": "Rose makes meaningful choices — refusing Cal, choosing Jack, surviving — but the inciting liberation is Jack's pursuit of her, and her survival at the climax depends heavily on Jack's sacrifice and others' rescue; strong but genuinely split between her choices and assembled rescue.", "core": "The film's emotional core is authentically about identity, freedom, class imprisonment, and love in the face of death — Rose's arc from suffocating conformity to selfhood is honest and resonant, earning the top band.", "music": "Celine Dion's 'My Heart Will Go On' is iconic but largely non-diegetic and end-credits placed; James Horner's score carries the film emotionally but original songs don't drive the narrative scene-by-scene the way Moana or Frozen songs do.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The film's world is almost entirely white and the class critique, while present, does not extend to race or broader inclusion; third-class passengers are used as backdrop tragedy without individuated humanity, a missed opportunity that constitutes passive exclusion.", "romance": "Being chosen by Jack — and the romantic union — is structurally the prize and the engine of Rose's liberation; marriage is not the endpoint but romantic selection absolutely is, placing this at 0."}

title: Forrest Gump
commentary: {"overall": "Forrest Gump is a deeply emotional film about innocence, love, and the randomness of fate, anchored by a protagonist who is genuinely ordinary in origin but whose life is repeatedly shaped by luck and historical coincidence as much as by his own choices. The emotional core is rich and honest — grief, unconditional love, belonging — but the structure endorses a kind of magical passivity where goodness is rewarded without a real grind, and Forrest accumulates wealth (shrimping empire, Apple stock) as a byproduct of stumbling through history rather than through earned struggle.", "wealth": "Forrest is born poor and low-status, which keeps him out of the bottom band, but the story endorses his ascension to considerable wealth (Bubba Gump Shrimp Co., accidental Apple investment) without critique or meaningful grind — it arrives as cosmic reward for being good, landing closer to the 20-band than the 40-band.", "agency": "Forrest makes genuine choices — running, joining the army, honoring Bubba, caring for Jenny — but many of his most consequential outcomes are luck-driven or historically accidental; he is present at events rather than driving them, placing him between the strong-agency and luck-heavy bands.", "core": "The emotional core is genuinely deep: unconditional love, grief over Jenny and Bubba and Mrs. Gump, the pain of not being chosen back, and the question of whether life is fate or chance — this is honest, substantial emotional territory at the level of the top band.", "music": "The film uses a licensed period soundtrack (CCR, Elvis, etc.) that is evocative and well-curated but carries no original songs and does no narrative heavy lifting beyond mood and era-setting.", "inclusion": "Band 5/10 (Neutral): The film features Black characters (Bubba, Lieutenant Dan's ancestors) with some dignity, and gestures at civil rights history, but the story is told entirely through a white protagonist's gaze and the Black characters exist primarily to serve his arc; no prejudice lesson is taught and no group is actively harmed as spectacle, landing at neutral.", "romance": "Jenny is a lifelong emotional obsession and Forrest does eventually marry her, but the film does not frame marriage as the prize — it frames it as bittersweet, brief, and ultimately about loss; romance is present but balanced against grief and acceptance."}

title: The Matrix
commentary: {"overall": "The Matrix is a structurally rich film whose emotional core — identity, reality, free will vs. determinism — is genuinely deep and honest. Neo is born-special in a way the narrative leans into heavily (The One prophecy), but he earns his awakening through choice and sacrifice; however, the prophecy scaffolding keeps him from full agency. Romance is present but secondary to the main arc.", "wealth": "Neo is an ordinary office worker/hacker with no inherited wealth or status, but the story's engine is a prophecy of born-into greatness (The One), which does meaningful narrative work without being fully examined as hollow — landing between the 20 (unexamined born-into greatness) and 35 bands, closer to 20 given how central the prophecy is to his specialness.", "agency": "Neo makes the pivotal choice to take the red pill and ultimately chooses to believe in himself over the Oracle's apparent verdict, but Morpheus, Trinity, and the prophecy framework all heavily scaffold his arc — the climax is partly destiny-assisted and partly self-willed, placing him firmly in the split/diluted band.", "core": "The film's emotional core is a genuinely profound meditation on identity, reality, free will, and self-belief — 'you have to let it all go, Neo — fear, doubt, and disbelief' — earning the top band.", "music": "The film relies on a licensed/composed score and licensed rock tracks (no original story-carrying songs), placing it squarely in the score-only/licensed soundtrack band.", "inclusion": "Band 7/10 (Meaningful representation, not the lesson): The ensemble is notably diverse in race and gender for a 1999 blockbuster — Tank, Morpheus, Switch, Trinity all carry real weight — but anti-prejudice is not the film's lesson.", "romance": "Trinity's love for Neo is narratively functional (it resurrects him at the climax), making romance present and plot-relevant but clearly subordinate to the identity/reality arc — balanced, not the prize."}

title: The Dark Knight
commentary: {"overall": "The Dark Knight is a morally serious crime epic whose emotional core — can a good man hold a line without becoming what he fights? — is genuinely deep and honest. However, Bruce Wayne's entire capacity to act is predicated on inherited billions, and the story never critiques that foundation; it simply uses it as infrastructure, landing the film near the Aladdin/Goodfellas band rather than the Cars band. Agency is strong but split: Batman makes the key moral choices, yet the Joker drives nearly every plot beat and Harvey Dent's arc resolves the thematic argument as much as Bruce's does.", "wealth": "Bruce Wayne is a billionaire by birth whose wealth is the unexamined engine of all his capability; the story never critiques or earns this — it is pure inherited-greatness-as-infrastructure, placing it near the 0–10 band (Goodfellas 6, Aladdin 5).", "agency": "Batman makes the defining moral choices (the sonar machine, the final lie) but the Joker architects virtually every situation and Dent's tragedy resolves the thematic core, making this a strong-but-split case rather than a clean 17.", "core": "The film's emotional core — whether a hero can maintain ethical limits under existential pressure, and what it costs society when he cannot — is as honest and deep as any in the rubric's top tier, comparable to Inside Out or Frozen in seriousness of intent.", "music": "Hans Zimmer and James Newton Howard provide a powerful but purely instrumental score with no original songs; score-only lands at 5.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The film's world is overwhelmingly male and white with no meaningful representation and no prejudice lesson; Rachel Dawes exists primarily as motivation for male characters, a missed opportunity that constitutes passive exclusion rather than active harm.", "romance": "Romance is entirely peripheral and ultimately killed off as a plot device rather than presented as a prize; it functions closer to subversion-by-elimination, earning a 6."}

title: American Gangster
commentary: {"overall": "American Gangster is a dual-protagonist crime epic in which Frank Lucas's ascension from driver to Harlem drug kingpin is the narrative engine, and the story endorses his empire-building with considerable glamour before a late moral reckoning that feels structurally insufficient to offset the glorification. The film sits firmly in the Scarface/Goodfellas band: status and wealth accumulation are the spectacle, and the climax is essentially a fall rather than a repudiation of the endorsed dream.", "wealth": "Frank Lucas is self-made but the story's primary dramatic energy is invested in his rise to wealth and power — the fur coats, the Thanksgiving dinners, the 'Blue Magic' brand — placing it squarely in the 0-6 band where status ascension is the endorsed reward regardless of whether it was inherited or climbed.", "agency": "Both Lucas and Richie Roberts drive their own arcs through deliberate choices, but the story is split between two protagonists and Lucas's ultimate resolution depends heavily on his cooperation deal — a structural assist — so strong-but-split agency lands at 13 rather than 17.", "core": "There is a real thematic undercurrent about integrity, institutional corruption, and the American Dream's dark mirror, but it remains largely surface-level in execution — the film is more interested in procedural spectacle than deep emotional excavation, placing it at the standard adventure/crime-drama beat level.", "music": "The film uses a licensed period soul and funk soundtrack that effectively evokes the 1970s Harlem setting but carries no original narrative songs; score-and-licensed-soundtrack band applies.", "inclusion": "Band 2/10 (Harmful stereotyping present): The film's Black characters are largely confined to criminal or victim roles while white institutional corruption is treated as a systemic backdrop rather than equal spectacle; the narrative othering of the Harlem community as a drug market, combined with the near-total absence of non-criminal Black agency, constitutes harmful framing present in the text.", "romance": "Romance is present — Lucas's relationship with Eva and Roberts's failed marriage — but neither is framed as the prize; both are subordinate to the crime and law-enforcement arcs, landing in the balanced middle band."}

title: The Godfather
commentary: {"overall": "The Godfather is a story about Michael Corleone's transformation from ordinary outsider to crime patriarch, with the dynasty's power and status as the unambiguous structural reward — the film ends with him enthroned as Don, doors closing, the family empire intact. The narrative glorifies the ascension even as it hints at moral cost, placing it firmly in the low-wealth band. Agency is strong but split across Michael and Vito, and the emotional core — identity, loyalty, corruption of the soul — has real substance without reaching the deepest introspective registers.", "wealth": "Status ascension is the endorsed structural outcome: Michael begins as the 'civilian' son and ends inheriting and consolidating the criminal empire, placing this squarely in the 0–10 band alongside Scarface and Goodfellas.", "agency": "Michael drives his own transformation through deliberate, consequential choices (volunteering to meet Sollozzo, ordering the hits, consolidating power), but the story is ensemble-heavy and Vito's legacy and the family machinery do significant narrative work alongside him.", "core": "The film has genuine substance in its exploration of loyalty, corruption, and the cost of power — Michael's soul-loss is a real arc — but it is more a crime saga than a deep interior emotional journey, landing at the 'real substance, simpler arc' band.", "music": "Nino Rota's iconic score carries enormous atmospheric weight but there are no story-carrying original songs; this is score-only territory.", "inclusion": "Band 2/10 (Harmful stereotyping present): The film's portrayal of Italian-Americans is bound up with Mafia criminality as ethnic identity, and its treatment of women is consistently othering — they are kept ignorant, decorative, or peripheral by the story's own endorsed logic.", "romance": "Romance is present (Michael's marriages) but is clearly subordinate to the power and loyalty themes; it is neither the prize nor actively subverted."}

title: Goodfellas
commentary: {"overall": "Goodfellas is a status-ascension story where the endorsed spectacle is the glamour of mob wealth and power, even as the ending nominally punishes Henry — the film spends 90% of its runtime glorifying the lifestyle it barely critiques. The structure endorses the climb far more than the fall, placing it firmly in the low-wealth band. Agency is real but destiny-assisted by Henry's mob connections and luck, and the emotional core is more about identity-through-belonging than deep honest grief.", "wealth": "Status ascension is the engine and spectacle of the narrative; the 'punishment' ending is perfunctory compared to the extended glorification of mob wealth, landing it near the Scarface/Aladdin band (scored 6, slightly above Scarface's 4 for the thin ironic framing Scorsese provides).", "agency": "Henry drives most of his own choices — joining the mob, the Lufthansa heist, flipping — but luck, circumstance, and the mob infrastructure around him dilute pure self-determination, placing him in the strong-but-split band.", "core": "There is real substance in the identity arc — Henry's lifelong need to belong to something that makes him feel special — but it is simpler and less emotionally excavated than a top-band film, landing at 13.", "music": "The film uses an iconic licensed rock soundtrack rather than original songs or a composed score doing narrative heavy lifting; score-only/licensed band applies.", "inclusion": "Band 2/10 (Harmful stereotyping present): Italian-American and Irish characters are portrayed through ethnic mob stereotypes that are played for authenticity and cool rather than critique; women are largely props or shrews, and the text does not interrogate these framings.", "romance": "Karen is a significant presence and the marriage is depicted with some complexity (her complicity, the affairs), making it balanced rather than 'being chosen as prize,' but it is clearly subordinate to the male status arc."}

title: Scarface
commentary: {"overall": "Scarface is a cautionary-tale-in-structure but the film's narrative energy overwhelmingly glorifies Tony Montana's violent ascension to drug-lord wealth and power, with his destruction framed as tragic rather than deserved — the story endorses the climb even as it punishes the climber. The emotional core is thin beneath the spectacle, and the reward structure is pure status acquisition. The rubric scores the endorsed outcome, and the film spends two-thirds of its runtime making Tony's rise look magnificent.", "wealth": "Scores in the 0-band: Tony is a penniless refugee whose entire story arc is the pursuit and achievement of extreme wealth and criminal status, presented as aspirational spectacle — self-made ascension to endorsed status-as-reward lands squarely at the bottom of the scale, per the rubric's explicit note that self-made ascension is penalised equally with inherited ascension.", "agency": "Tony drives virtually every plot decision himself — he chooses his alliances, executes his rivals, and engineers his own rise and fall — but his climactic last stand is partly destiny-assisted bravado and the resolution is death, so it sits in the strong-but-split band rather than a full 17.", "core": "The film gestures at themes of the American Dream's corruption and immigrant ambition, but these remain surface-level; the actual emotional engine is power-lust and paranoia rather than a deep honest reckoning with identity or belonging, placing it in the standard-adventure/spectacle band.", "music": "Giorgio Moroder's synthesizer score is atmospheric and period-defining but there are no story-carrying songs; the soundtrack functions as pure score/licensed-adjacent mood-setting, landing at the score-only band.", "inclusion": "Band 2/10 (Harmful stereotyping present in the text): Cuban and Latin characters are broadly rendered as violent criminals or corrupt officials, Elvira is a trophy object, and the film's world traffics in ethnic and gender stereotypes that are present and unremarked-upon in the text — consistent with the rubric's Scarface anchor of 2.", "romance": "Elvira is partly a status trophy Tony acquires on the way up, but the marriage deteriorates and is not presented as the prize — romance is present and transactional but not the structural reward, placing it in the balanced middle band."}

title: The Wolf of Wall Street
commentary: {"overall": "The Wolf of Wall Street is a maximalist portrait of status-as-reward: Jordan Belfort climbs from nothing but the film's structure glorifies the ascent and the excess, never genuinely condemning it — the fourth-wall winks and the audience-as-marks final shot endorse the spectacle rather than critique it. Agency is high because Belfort drives every scene, but the emotional core is shallow hedonism dressed as satire, and women and minorities exist almost exclusively as props or punchlines.", "wealth": "Scores in the 0-band: self-made ascension is still penalised when status and wealth are the endorsed reward, and the film's entire narrative engine is the glorification of that climb — the rubric explicitly cites this film at score 2.", "agency": "Belfort is relentlessly active and drives his own story through his own choices at every turn, but the resolution is partly externally imposed (the FBI closes in) rather than a fully self-authored conclusion, landing in the strong-but-destiny/externally-assisted band.", "core": "The surface is excess and crime, and while there are gestures toward emptiness and self-destruction, the film never excavates a genuinely honest emotional core — it remains at the level of standard rise-and-fall spectacle rather than grief, identity, or fear-vs-love.", "music": "No original story-carrying songs; the film uses a licensed period soundtrack (Lemonheads, Billy Joel, etc.) as atmosphere rather than narrative engine, placing it in the score-only/licensed band.", "inclusion": "Band 0/10 (Active degradation as spectacle): Women are systematically degraded as sexual objects and status props throughout; the Quaalude-fuelled set pieces, the 'chest-drumming' scene, and the near-total absence of non-white characters with interiority constitute active othering performed as spectacle for the audience's entertainment — the rubric explicitly cites this film at 0.", "romance": "Marriage and women are purely transactional status symbols for Belfort — Naomi is literally a trophy acquisition and the second marriage is framed as an upgrade in the status hierarchy, making being-chosen-as-prize the operative logic throughout."}

title: The Princess and the Frog
commentary: {"overall": "Tiana is a self-made protagonist whose dream is a restaurant earned through relentless labor, not status — the rubric's own anchor example. The romance is present but balanced against her work ethic, and the emotional core is a genuine meditation on ambition, grief (her father's death), and what 'almost there' really costs. The film scores high across most dimensions because the story's endorsed reward is her livelihood, not the prince.", "wealth": "Tiana is a working-class Black woman whose entire arc is about earning her dream through labor; the prince is broke and the reward is the restaurant, not status — the rubric explicitly anchors this film at 34.", "agency": "Tiana drives nearly every key decision — she pursues the wish, she figures out Facilier's scheme, she chooses love — but Naveen's parallel arc and the climax's shared resolution dilute sole agency slightly below 17.", "core": "The story is genuinely about the cost of single-minded ambition and what her late father's lesson really meant — real emotional substance, though slightly simpler than the deepest grief/identity arcs of Coco or Inside Out.", "music": "Randy Newman's original songs ('Almost There,' 'Friends on the Other Side,' 'Down in New Orleans') carry character, theme, and plot in the classic Disney tradition — full marks.", "inclusion": "Band 8/10 (strong inclusion content, secondary to main arc): The film centers a Black female protagonist in a historically white Disney canon and engages meaningfully with New Orleans culture, but anti-prejudice is not the explicit lesson the way it is in Zootopia or Mulan.", "romance": "Romance is present and Naveen is a genuine partner, but the story's emotional and narrative weight rests on Tiana's dream and her father's legacy — the prince is not the prize."}

title: Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory
commentary: {"overall": "Charlie is a genuinely poor, honest kid whose virtue is rewarded — but the reward is inheriting an entire factory and fortune, which the rubric explicitly places in the low band regardless of how sympathetic the pursuit. The film has real emotional warmth and memorable songs, but the endorsed outcome is pure status ascension via being chosen by a wealthy eccentric.", "wealth": "The rubric explicitly names this film (Willy Wonka 15) as a case where a poor honest kid whose reward is INHERITING THE FACTORY lands in the low band; I score 14 because the 'being chosen' mechanism is even more passive than a self-made climb — Charlie does nothing to earn the factory except not steal a gobstopper.", "agency": "Charlie makes one pivotal moral choice (returning the Everlasting Gobstopper) that determines the outcome, but the contest structure, the golden ticket lottery, and Wonka's hidden test all frame the resolution as something assembled around Charlie rather than driven by him — split credit between his choice and Wonka's design puts this between 8 and 13.", "core": "Beneath the fantasy spectacle is a genuine theme about integrity and goodness in the face of poverty and temptation — real substance, though it is delivered more as moral fable than deep emotional excavation.", "music": "Original songs ('Pure Imagination,' 'The Candy Man,' 'I've Got a Golden Ticket') are woven directly into the story's emotional and narrative fabric, earning the top band.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The world is presented as a default homogeneous setting with no harmful stereotyping but no meaningful representation or anti-prejudice lesson; the Oompa Loompas as an imported exotic workforce is a missed-opportunity-at-best, passive-harm-at-worst element that keeps this from a 5.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent from the narrative; the prize is the factory, not a partner."}
